stories.title: Korda Leads Chevron Championship by 5 — Final Round Today for 3rd Major Title

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text:
Nelly Korda enters the Chevron Championship final round with a FIVE-SHOT LEAD at -16.

Tavatanakit is at -11. Yin and Roussin-Bouchard are at -10.

Korda has a chance to win her 3rd major title today in Houston.

#GolfFanrecap #ChevronChampionship #LPGATour

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text:
Korda in the final pairing at the Chevron Championship. 5-shot lead. Sunday. Houston.

She won here in 2021. She won the U.S. Women's Open in 2024.

Today could be No. 3.

#GolfFanrecap #ChevronChampionship #LPGATour

facebook.long_form:
Window: 8:00 AM ET

Nelly Korda is five shots away from her third major championship.

The world's No. 1 women's golfer enters the final round of the 2026 Chevron Championship at 16-under par, with a five-stroke advantage over Pajaree Anannarukarn (Patty T.) at 11-under. Ruoning Yin and Pauline Roussin-Bouchard are tied at 10-under in third, and Inbee Yoon is fifth at 8-under. The tournament is at Memorial Park Golf Course in Houston, and $9 million is on the line.

Korda has been unrelenting. She opened the week with back-to-back bogey-free 65s — a standard of play that put the rest of the field immediately on notice. She backed that up with a third round that kept her firmly in command. She has played this tournament before and knows what winning here means: she took the Chevron title in 2021, and added the U.S. Women's Open in 2024. A win today would be her third major, cementing a legacy that is already historic for players of her generation.

Tavatanakit and Yin are capable of hot rounds, and foursomes format can produce momentum shifts in an instant. But Korda enters Sunday with the experience, the form, and the lead. The burden of proof rests with the chasers.

#GolfFanrecap | #LPGATour

article.headline: Korda Enters Chevron Championship Final Round With 5-Shot Lead, Chasing Third Major

stories.title: Fitzpatrick Brothers Enter Zurich Final Round With 4-Shot Lead After Historic 57

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text:
Fitzpatrick brothers enter the Zurich Classic final round with a 4-SHOT LEAD.

Record 57 on Saturday. Matt won RBC Heritage last week. Final round foursomes today at TPC Louisiana.

#GolfFanrecap #ZurichClassic #PGATour

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text:
Matt and Alex Fitzpatrick lead the Zurich Classic by 4. Final round today.

Matt is World No. 3 after winning RBC Heritage last week. Alex is matching him step for step.

Brothers. Champions?

#GolfFanrecap #ZurichClassic #PGATour

facebook.long_form:
Window: 9:00 AM ET

What a week it has been for the Fitzpatrick family.

Matt Fitzpatrick — World No. 3 after winning the RBC Heritage just last week — and his brother Alex enter the final round of the Zurich Classic of New Orleans with a four-shot lead at 30-under par. They earned it in extraordinary fashion on Saturday: their 15-under 57 in the fourball round was a tournament record, making birdie or better on 14 of 18 holes at TPC Louisiana. The previous tournament record of 58 had stood for all of one day before the Fitzpatricks made it look ordinary.

Alex Smalley and Hayden Springer are tied with Davis Thompson and Austin Eckroat at 26-under in second place. Doug Ghim and Jeffrey Kang are at 25-under, five back. Sunday's format is foursomes — alternate shot — which demands precision and communication. It is unforgiving. A single bad hole can swing the momentum of an entire round.

The Fitzpatricks have shown they can handle the format: they shot 65 in the alternate shot round on Friday during the tournament's second round. They have the experience, the sibling connection, and Matt's red-hot form from Hilton Head. A Zurich Classic win would be one of the most heartwarming moments of the 2026 golf season.

#GolfFanrecap | #PGATour

stories.title: What a Korda Chevron Win Would Mean — A Third Major for Golf's Most Dominant Player

x_posts.label: Post 3-1
x_posts.text:
Chevron 2021. U.S. Women's Open 2024. Chevron 2026?

If Nelly Korda closes today with a 5-shot lead, she'll have 3 major titles before her 28th birthday.

One of the great careers in modern LPGA history — and it keeps getting better.

#GolfFanrecap #LPGATour #ChevronChampionship

x_posts.label: Post 3-2
x_posts.text:
Nelly Korda entered this Chevron Championship final round bogey-free across her first two rounds.

Back-to-back 65s. A 5-shot lead. A chance to be a 3-time major winner.

This is why she's the face of the LPGA right now.

#GolfFanrecap #LPGATour #ChevronChampionship

facebook.long_form:
Window: 3:30 PM ET

It is easy to say Nelly Korda is the best women's golfer in the world. It is harder to appreciate just how unprecedented her run of excellence has been — and what a victory at today's Chevron Championship would mean in historical terms.

Korda won the Chevron Championship in 2021, becoming one of the few players to win the event in a non-Augusta-National format. She won the U.S. Women's Open in 2024 with a performance that drew comparisons to the great wire-to-wire victories in major championship history. Now she stands five shots ahead in the final round of the 2026 Chevron, with a chance to win her third major before her 28th birthday.

In the modern LPGA era — post-2010, as the field has become increasingly international and competitive — only a handful of players have won three or more majors before age 28. Korda is on the verge of joining that group. She has been ranked World No. 1 for extended stretches over the past two seasons, won on Tour at a rate that leaves competitors searching for explanations, and played the opening rounds of this Chevron without a single bogey — a standard of consistency that speaks to how locked in she is right now.

What she is building in Houston today is not just a win. It is a career-defining moment that will be cited for decades when the conversation turns to the greatest LPGA players of the current generation.

#GolfFanrecap | #LPGATour

article.headline: What a Third Major Would Mean for Nelly Korda — And Why the LPGA Should Cherish This Moment

stories.title: Zurich Classic Final Round: Can Anyone Chase Down the Fitzpatrick Brothers?

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
Zurich Classic final round: Fitzpatrick brothers lead by 4, but foursomes (alternate shot) is the format where big leads can disappear fast.

Smalley/Springer and Thompson/Eckroat are 4 back. Anything can happen.

TPC Louisiana. Let's go.

#GolfFanrecap #ZurichClassic #PGATour

facebook.long_form:
Window: 10:30 AM ET

Four shots in foursomes is not the same as four shots in stroke play. The alternate shot format — where partners alternate hitting the same ball, sharing both the glory and the consequence of every shot — means that one bad swing, one awkward lie, one missed short putt can swing a round in moments. It is why Sunday's Zurich Classic final round will be compelling even with the Fitzpatricks holding a seemingly comfortable lead.

Alex Smalley and Hayden Springer, who set a tournament record with a 58 in Thursday's first round, are four back at 26-under. Davis Thompson and Austin Eckroat are tied with them. Doug Ghim and Jeffrey Kang are five back at 25-under. Any of these teams is capable of a run in foursomes — a format that can produce eagles when chemistry is right and train wrecks when it isn't.

The Fitzpatricks' edge is their history with each other and with the format. Matt and Alex have practiced together, competed together in team formats, and built the kind of intuitive understanding that alternate shot demands. Matt's form heading into the week was elite — World No. 3, fresh off a win at Harbour Town — and Alex has matched that level all week. Their record 57 Saturday was not a surprise to them. They are playing their best golf at the right time.

Four shots is not a guarantee. But the Fitzpatricks have earned the right to be considered very heavy favorites to close it out.

#GolfFanrecap | #PGATour

stories.title: LIV Golf Virginia — What to Watch at Trump National DC (May 8-10)

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text:
LIV Golf heads to Trump National DC in Virginia, May 8-10.

Rahm just won Mexico City by 6. Niemann is the defending individual champion. DeChambeau's Crushers are the defending team champs.

This one has multiple storylines to watch.

#GolfFanrecap #LIVGolf

facebook.long_form:
Window: 8:30 AM ET

The LIV Golf calendar moves to the Washington area next, with Trump National Golf Club in Sterling, Virginia hosting the circuit's May event from the 8th through the 10th — and there are several compelling storylines to follow as the season heats up.

Jon Rahm is the man of the moment. He just won the Mexico City individual title with a final-round 7-under 64 at Club de Golf Chapultepec, winning by six shots in a dominant display that demonstrated why he remains one of the world's most complete ball-strikers. Rahm and Legion XIII have now claimed three consecutive team titles — an unprecedented run in LIV's relatively short history. He arrives in Virginia as the form player in the field.

But Joaquín Niemann is the defending individual champion at this specific event, and the Crushers GC — Bryson DeChambeau's team — are the defending team champions. Niemann's game is ideally suited to Trump National's layout, which demands precise iron play and patience off the tee. DeChambeau and the Crushers will be motivated to reclaim team honors after Legion XIII's Mexico City dominance.

In the overall team standings, Ripper GC leads with 89.75 points, 4Aces GC is close behind with 88, and Legion XIII is third following their Mexico City title.

#GolfFanrecap | #LIVGolf

article.headline: LIV Golf Virginia Preview: Rahm on a Roll, Niemann Defends, and a Title Race Heats Up
